preFix.title: Goat fencing and escape prevention
preFix.subtitle: Why standard sheep fencing fails for goats, and what actually keeps them in.
preFix.excerpt: Goats escape from fencing that holds sheep and cattle without difficulty. They climb, lever, and squeeze through gaps, and once one learns a route, the rest follow. A guide to the fencing specifications that work for goats, the most common failure points, and how to audit an existing fence before the animals arrive.
preFix.sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Goats, Crown copyright. SAC Consulting Scotland, goat management guidance. AHDB Beef and Lamb open-access smallholder factsheets.

content.text: A goat will test every section of its fence within the first 24 hours. It is not malice; it is curiosity, and a goat that finds a weakness will use it. Fencing that was fine for sheep or cattle will not reliably hold goats. The baseline requirement is stock netting at least 1.1 m tall with an electric strand at 50 cm above the fence top.
Why goats escape where sheep do not

Goats climb, lean, and push in a way that sheep rarely do. A goat will put its front hooves on the top of a fence to reach vegetation on the other side and then walk along it, stressing the posts. It will push its head through a gap and use its body weight to widen it. Young goats, especially kids, will squeeze through any horizontal gap wider than their heads.

Goats also learn from each other. One escape route found by one animal will be used by the whole group within days. The first goat that learns the fence near the gate can be walked on earns a reputation for it and teaches it to every animal in the pen.

at 1.1 m height with maximum 15 cm horizontal gaps at the base. Posts at no more than 2 m spacing, driven a minimum of 60 cm into the ground. A full concrete or rammed stake at corners and at intervals along any run longer than 50 m. The netting stapled or clipped close to the post at every height, with no drooping sections.

on an offset bracket 10 to 15 cm out from the top of the netting and around 50 cm above it. This stops leaning and climbing. A second strand along the base at 20 cm stops pushing and digging under. Run both strands from the same energiser. Check voltage weekly.

attrs.body: Gates are the most common escape point. A galvanised gate that hangs 15 cm off the ground gives a goat enough room to squirm under it. Hang gates tight to the ground or fit a board or additional rail across the bottom gap. Latch with a two-stage catch; goats can work open a single-lever latch by lifting it repeatedly.
attrs.title: Gates need the same standard

content.text: Walk the perimeter before the goats arrive. Check every post for movement when pushed firmly. A loose post in soft ground will fail within days of goats leaning on the fence above it. Tap posts at the ground line; a hollow sound indicates rot in a timber post.

Look for any section where the netting has pulled away from the post at the top. This is where leaning starts. Staple back tightly. Check for low spots where the fence dips between posts; a goat will walk the fence line looking for these. A tight brace wire along the top rail of the netting at fence height stops sagging in long runs.
Managing vegetation along the fence line
Clear vegetation from within 30 cm of the fence on the inside.

that can reach browse through the fence from inside press against the wire constantly, loosening it over time. Trim any tree branches that overhang the fence from outside; a branch at fence height becomes a stepping stone.

items.fix: Watch the goat from a distance to find the exit route rather than repairing sections at random. The goat will return to the same point. Fix that point with a re-driven post or doubled wire.
items.cause: A section you did not spot, or a post that was looser than it appeared on inspection
items.symptom: Goat escaping despite apparently sound fencing

items.fix: Check voltage with a fence tester. The strand must be at least 4 cm clear of the top wire on its offset bracket at all points. A contact between strand and netting earths the current.
items.cause: Voltage too low, or the strand is earthing through the fence it runs beside

items.fix: Add a second run of smaller-gauge netting (7 cm aperture) along the base section to 40 cm height. A single strand of electric at 15 cm also deters pushing through at the base.
items.cause: Gaps in the mesh too large for young kids; standard sheep netting has 15 cm base gaps
items.symptom: Kids escaping through the base of the netting

initialFindings.message: Tutorial PUBLISHED with heroMediaId IS NULL — hero-fill must be run

tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
